coredata.dc:description: Generally, hyperthermia is referred to the composites capability to increase local temperature in such a way that the generated heat would lead to cancerous or bacteria cells destruction, with minimum damage to normal tissue cells. Many different materials have been utilized for hyperthermia application via different heat generating methods. Carbon-based nanomaterials consisting of graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT), carbon dot (CD) and carbon quantum dot (CQD), nanodiamond (ND), fullerene and carbon fiber (CF), have been studied significantly for different applications including hyperthermia due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, chemical and physical stability, thermal and electrical conductivity and in some cases photothermal conversion. Therefore, in this comprehensive review, a structure-based view on carbon nanomaterials application in hyperthermia therapy of cancer and bacteria via various methods such as optical, magnetic, ultrasonic and radiofrequency-induced hyperthermia is presented.

head.ce:title: Recent advances on hyperthermia therapy applications of carbon-based nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Generally, hyperthermia is referred to the composites capability to increase local temperature in such a way that the generated heat would lead to cancerous or bacteria cells destruction, with minimum damage to normal tissue cells. Many different materials have been utilized for hyperthermia application via different heat generating methods. Carbon-based nanomaterials consisting of graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT), carbon dot (CD) and carbon quantum dot (CQD), nanodiamond (ND), fullerene and carbon fiber (CF), have been studied significantly for different applications including hyperthermia due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, chemical and physical stability, thermal and electrical conductivity and in some cases photothermal conversion. Therefore, in this comprehensive review, a structure-based view on carbon nanomaterials application in hyperthermia therapy of cancer and bacteria via various methods such as optical, magnetic, ultrasonic and radiofrequency-induced hyperthermia is presented.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In a simple and comprehensive definition, hyperthermia means overheating due to external factors, in which body temperature increases abnormally and causes the body's heat-regulation system to fail. It is confirmed that the increasing unusual temperature is a destructive factor for normal body tissues, but it can be used as an effective method to eliminate cancer cells. As a dangerous disease, cancer is considered the second cause of death in the world, for this reason, several studies are conducted on its treatment methods, one of which is hyperthermia. Tumor eradication with this technique is commonly known as thermal treatment or thermotherapy. In the type of treatment, the temperature of a specific region of the body enhances in such a way that cancer cells are destroyed with minimal damage to healthy body tissues through controlling temperature [1]. Considering the time and range of temperature, hyperthermia affects cancer cells with a different mechanism. An increase in temperature up to 46 °C commonly stimulates the immune system and induces cancer cells to undergo apoptosis, while an increase in temperature from 46 °C to 56 °C directly affects tumor structure [2].

Moreover, hyperthermia is classified into different sorts based on the conversion of energy sources such as magnetic field, ultrasound, microwave, laser (ultraviolet, visible, near-infrared light (NIR)), radio frequency, etc [3–6]. Commonly, the combination of hyperthermia with other cancer treatment methods (e.g., surgery, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and photodynamic therapy) will multiply the effectiveness of the treatment [7–9]. In addition to the use of hyperthermia in cancer treatment, increasing the temperature in other applications is also considered. For instance, nanotechnology-based hyperthermia has a specific place in various applications mainly diagnosis, sensing, tissue engineering, water treatment, anti-icing/deicing usage.

Recently carbon-based nanostructures including graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNTs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), fullerene, nanodiamonds (NDs), carbon fibers (CFs), etc. are widely utilized as a new generation of nanomaterials for hyperthermic approaches [10]. The use of carbon nanomaterials enhances the efficiency of hyperthermia due to their unique physiochemical properties such as thermal, electrical, optical, mechanical, high aspect ratio, easy functionalization, excellent cell penetration, nanoscale size, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and existence high functional groups [11,12].

Among different carbon nanostructures, GO and graphene-based nanomaterials with high capability of NIR light conversion act as common photothermic agents through plasmonic effects [13]. When these conductive nanomaterials are irradiated with a specific energy of NIR light that matches their resonance frequency, the light is absorbed and heat is emitted around. The penetration of light through the tissue makes the carbon-based nanomaterials good candidates for the treatment of non-superficial cancerous lesions inside the body, while surgery for these types of cancers has its own problems [14]. Furthermore, the photothermal therapy as known a technique of hyperthermia therapy, exhibits several advantages such as reduced side effects, minimal invasiveness, and high specificity [15]. A key advantage of photothermal therapy over other approaches is its ability to bypass problems related to magnetic agents including dental fillings, crowns and implants in the targeted region which poses challenges in the context of magnetic hyperthermia therapy [16].

Also, different forms of CNTs, as another member of carbon nanomaterials, have the ability to absorb a wide range of electromagnetic spectrum such as NIR Ⅰ, NIR Ⅱ, microwave, and radio frequency [17]. The specific wavelength absorbance and efficiency of energy conversion are directly related to the unique physical structure of CNTs including length, diameter, and the number of cylindrical nanosheets [18]. The capacity CNTs to absorb a broad spectrum of electromagnetic waves represents a notable advantage over other types of nanoparticles, which absorb only a specific range of wavelengths that are contingent upon their respective size and shape [16].

Fullerene similar to CNTs raises surrounding temperature under microwave irradiation through electron-phonon and phonon-phonon interactions [19,20]. Furthermore, CQDs and CDs are photoresponsive agents due to π-electrons of carbon and unpaired electrons of surface heteroatoms, which convert the absorbed photons to heat or lower energy photons [21], so these photoluminescent and phototherapeutic nanomaterials are extensively used in bioimaging, biosensing, diagnosis and cancer therapy applications [22–25]. An important issue that should be considered in the thermotherapy of cancer cells is the solubility and water dispersibility of carbon nanostructures which is resolved by easy functionalization of carbon nanostructures [26,27]. Besides, the high potential of carbon-based nanomaterials for incorporation with various magnetic nanoparticles, sonosensitizer, and photosentisizer agents via the formation of covalent and noncovalent interactions between different surface functional groups enhance the hyperthermia performance and techniques variety [28–30]. It can be concluded; nanocomposites based on functionalized hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials effectively mitigates the disadvantages of used agents (e.g. aggregation) by virtue of their high dispensability, thereby enhancing their overall performance [31]. Considering the advantages that carbon nanomaterials have over other nanoparticles, it should be noted that clinical trials on carbon nanomaterials have not yet been performed and the delayed effects of these nanomaterials should be investigated and clinical trials should be launched for human trials [32].

In this comprehensive review, the structure of carbon nanomaterials and their physicochemical properties have been explained, then the hyperthermia applications, the methods used, and the effective mechanisms have been described, and finally composites synthesized with carbon-based nanomaterials and recent research in this field are introduced.

Carbon nanostructures have shown great potential for biomedical applications in terms of drug delivery, gene delivery, biolabeling, tissue engineering, wound healing, hyperthermia, and cancer treatment. [33]. However, their biocompatibility is a matter of controversy and requires attention in order to avoid unintended toxicity.

Since carbon is hydrophobic in nature, untreated carbon nanostructures are also hydrophobic. Hydrophobic interactions of carbon nanostructures with the cholesterol molecules of the cell membrane might lead to membrane damage, resulting in cytotoxicity [34]. To overcome this challenge, surface functionalization is commonly used. Carbon nanostructures are easy to functionalize and this action can aid with increasing their biocompatibility and reducing the cytotoxicity [35–37].

Moreover, carbon nanostructures toxicity strongly depends on the applied dose. For instance, test results have indicated that GO can cause cell viability reduction at high concentrations. In vivo experiments have shown that at high doses (0.4 mg) lung granuloma and death of mice occurred, while no apparent toxicity was observed using lower doses (0.1–0.25 mg) [38].

Another important factor that influences the carbon nanostructures toxicity is their preparation method. For instant, using chemical oxidizers and reducing agent such as toxic hydrazine reducing agent in GO and rGO preparation, increases their toxicity toward cells and causes apoptosis [39]. The best way to overcome this challenge, is to use green synthetic approaches. In addition, a very common method to minimize the toxicity of carbon nanostructures is to immobilize them in a polymer matrix and form a polymer/carbon nanostructure nanocomposite, which significantly reduces toxicity. [40].

Tests on immune system response to carbon nanostructures also indicates the importance of functionalization. For instance, it was demonstrated that CNTs can activate the first defense line of innate immune system [41]. However, Dumortier et al. indicated that after functionalization, CNTs preserve the primary immune cells functionality and do not influence the B and T lymphocytes and macrophages cell viability after uptake [42]. Experiments shows that NDs do not stimulate the immune responses and do not alter macrophages functionality and also, show negligible cytotoxicity to macrophages [43].

Studies have indicated that carbon nanomaterials biodegradability strongly depends on their surface properties [44]. Graphene degrades by neutrophile myeloperoxidase and CNTs can biodegrade through enzymatic oxidation by macrophages and the byproducts were reported to be non-toxic in vivo and in vivo [45,46]. In the presence of lipase, CDs undergo proxied catalyzed degradation and produce hydroxymethyl furfural as a metabolic by-product [47].

To conclude, carbon-based nanomaterials biocompatibility highly depends on the preparation methods, size, surface functionalization, purity and hydrophilicity. By taking these items into consideration, carbon-based nanostructures can be used as biocompatible and biodegradable materials for biomedical applications such as hyperthermia in cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different methods and mechanisms of hyperthermia therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Hyperthermia property is referred to composites ability to increase local temperature. This capability can be utilized for cancerous or bacteria cells destruction with minimal damage to normal tissue cells. In cancer therapy application, hyperthermia can also increase cells sensitivity toward chemotherapy and radiotherapy [48]. Generally, hyperthermia can take place as magnetic heating [49] ultrasound heating, optical heating [50], radiofrequency and microwave hyperthermia [51,52].

In magnetic hyperthermia, a magnetic particle is placed in an external alternating magnetic field (AMF), causing heat via Neel/Brownian relaxation which leads to local temperature raise up to a certain level [53–55]. Ultrasonic waves can be utilized for ultrasound hyperthermia, with the advantage of high ability for focusing on a small region [28]. Sonodynamic hyperthermia can overcome the depth-penetration issue of photo-induced therapies [56]. Optical or photo-induced hyperthermia is referred to temperature raise under UV, IR and NIR laser irradiation [57]. Photo-induced hyperthermia, along with magnetic hyperthermia can be considered as the two most investigated hyperthermia methods.

In a mechanistic perspective, hyperthermia can directly affect cancer cells or cause drug release from a thermosensitive composite, leading to selective destruction of cancerous cells within the targeted tissue. Clinical studies have indicated that hyperthermia therapy in combination with other cancer therapy methods such as radiotherapy, leads to tumor shrinking and easer cancer cell destruction [58]. Generally, cancer cells are more vulnerable to heat than normal cells and undergo apoptosis at temperatures higher than 46 ℃ [59]. The cancer cell destruction mechanism under heat could be attributed to the membrane and cytoplasm proteins denaturation [60]. Also, it has been reported that at high temperatures, nutrient and oxygen deprivation occurs at tumor microenvironment factors due to the vasculature of the damaged structures of the tumor site and leads to the cancer cells destruction [61]. Common direct hyperthermia methods are magnetic hyperthermia which navigates magnetic nanoparticles in the presence of magnetic field into various directions, leading to heat generation caused by particles vibration and photo-induced hyperthermia which uses photo-absorbing agents and converts light into heat. Moreover, hyperthermia can trigger drug release from thermosensitive composites such as hydrogels, and lead to cancer cell destruction indirectly. Other than cancerous cells, bacteria are also vulnerable to high temperature and their proliferation and mobility is inhibited which increases their autolysis and cell wall damage [62]. Also, it was demonstrated that at temperatures above 46 ℃, the outer membrane of the Gram-negative E. coli is disrupted, leading to surviving bacteria reduction and 10 min exposure to 44–45 ℃ leads to protein synthesis decrease in the mentioned bacteria [63,64].

Hyperthermia can be combined with other therapeutic methods such as chemotherapy for enhanced therapy effect [65]. A different variety of nanomaterials can take part in hyperthermia application. Carbon nanoparticles have been investigated significantly for different biomedical applications due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, low toxicity and easy functionalization.

Carbon nanoparticles nature is responsive toward some hyperthermia methods. For instance, CDs are optically active and can take part in photothermal therapy (PTT). But carbon nanoparticles do not exhibit any special magnetic ability, therefore carbon-based composites used in magnetic hyperthermia, must be magnetized with other particles. By taking advantage of carbon-based nanomaterials easy functionalization property, they can be adjusted for hyperthermia methods in which they were inert, or even exhibit synergistic effects for those methods that they were naturally active [66,67]. The most common functionalization for magnetic hyperthermia are iron oxides Fe3O4 and γ-Fe2O3 which appear to be compatible with human body [68–70]. GO, CNT and other carbon nanomaterials have been extensively functionalized with iron oxide for magnetic hyperthermia [71–73].

An important factor in hyperthermia application, is heat transfer. The composite must be able to transfer the generated heat to the environment in a controlled manner. Therefore, materials used in hyperthermia must be heat conductive so that they would not trap the heat within their structure [74]. It has been demonstrated that carbon nanoparticles in fact, exhibit excellent heat conductivity and can be considered as great potential candidates for heat transfer and hyperthermia applications [75] GOs and CNTs thermal conductivity have been studied considerably during the past two decades [76–79].

Considering these particles great potential for hyperthermia applications via different methods, a comprehensive review on their capacity for hyperthermia is needed. Thus, this review focuses on the application of the carbon-based nanomaterials in hyperthermia with emphasis on cancer treatment and bacteria elimination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para: GO and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), are among the most studied carbon nanostructures in different applications[80,81]. This is due to their large surface area, ease of functionalization, high thermal conductivity and optical absorption [82,83]. These particles excellent photothermal conversion and optical absorption has turned them into great potential candidates for hyperthermia application [84,85].

However, because of GOs and rGOs large surface area and nanosheet structure, they aggregate easily in solutions and have low dispersibility in an aqueous media [86]. To overcome these issues and increase the biocompatibility of these particles, functionalization with other agents, such as polymers, have proven to be beneficial [87–89]. Jhang et al. Decorated rGO with PEGylated chitosan20k to overcome the poor dispersibility in environments containing serum. Photothermal conversion efficiency under 808 nm NIR laser was 67.7% with and in vitro cancerous cell elimination by over 49 °C photothermal hyperthermia was achieved. The reactive site toward laser irradiation in this study was rGO, considering the other components inert nature toward light [90]. Soysal et al. functionalized rGO with polypyrrole, which is a conductive polymer and designed rGO/Fe3O4 @PPy composite. A NIR laser at 1.5–3.0 W/cm2 was used as the source of irradiation for determining photothermal efficiency of the dispersed composite in aqueous media. The photothermal efficiency was found to be 68% and the nanocomposite showed capability for multiple times usage with no considerable loss in maximum obtained temperature [91].

Hyperthermia has proven to be an efficient method for killing bacteria [92]. Different studies have indicated the positive effect of hyperthermia biocidal ability in applications such as wound healing [93–95]. Federico et al. loaded a hyaluronic acid based (HA-EDA) electrospun membrane with GO and ciprofloxacin. Alongside photothermal activity under NIR laser, light-triggered drug release was also achieved. The synergistic effect of GO response to light which led to laser-induced hyperthermia and antibiotic loaded drug led to a decrease in staphylococcus aureus and pseudomonas aeruginosa pathogens viability [96]. In a research study, in order to use hyperthermia to disinfect water sources contaminated with bacteria, Zhang et al. fabricated Ni/rGO nanocomposite with diverse oxygen vacancy amounts for disinfection application. The nanocomposite exhibited an effective disinfection capability of 99.6% and 99.5% against E. coli and Bacillus subtilis under NIR irradiation for 8 min with synergic photothermal and oxidative damage. The observed conversion efficiency was as high as 35.78% under 808 nm NIR irradiation which had led to photo-induced hyperthermia and pathogen-contaminated water disinfection [97].

Silver NPs are known for their biocidal activity and have been studied considerably for biomedical applications [98,99]. Tan et al. utilized both rGO and Ag NPs excellent photothermal property and designed rGO/Ag nanocomposite for multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria. Under 0.30 W/cm2 NIR irradiation for 10 min, nearly 100% bacteria cell death was observed on both regular bacteria of E. coli and MDR bacteria of klebsiella pneumonia. In addition to the rGO and Ag NPs synergistic photothermal activity, photodynamic activity and ROS generation under NIR irradiation was also observed. At high concentrations, rGO/Ag nanocomposite demonstrated low cytotoxicity indicating the nanocomposites efficiency as an antibacterial agent against both regular and MDR bacteria types [100].

Combined with hyperthermia, detection methods can also be utilized for detecting the correct location of the bacteria and their proper elimination with hyperthermia in biological environments [101]. In a research study, Yang et al. designed a core-shell structure of Fe3O4-Au with poly-ethylenimine (PEI) modification. Further modifications were performed by attaching Fe3O4 NPs to poly (dimethyldiallylammonium chloride) (PDDA)- GO. The Fe3O4-Au@rGO composite was used for bacteria hyperthermia treatment and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) detection with 90% biocidal efficiency within 10 min [102].

Hyperthermia biocidal property could also be an interesting method for adding biocidal activity to biomedical implants. In a recent study, Chai et al. prepared porous TiO2 coatings using micro-arc oxidation with improved biological activity. Later modifications with GO were performed to prepare TiO2/GO coatings. The TiO2 alone was not responsive toward NIR irradiation but after GO addition, a significant antibacterial activity under 808-nm NIR irradiation was observed. In vivo and in vitro tests exhibited synergic photothermal and photodynamic effect of this coatings on Streptococcus mutans [103].

Considering the fact that cancerous cells are very sensitive towards heat, hyperthermia can be considered as an efficient way in cancer treatment [8104,105]. Eivazzadeh-Keihan et al. developed a novel magnetic nanocomposite by functionalizing GO with natural polymer lignin using epichlorohydrin cross-linker and then magnetized it with Fe3O4 NPs. Under AMF, magnetic hyperthermia was achieved with saturation magnetization value of 17.2 emu.g -1 and maximum specific absorption rate of 121.22 W.g-1 [10]. Barra et al. used chitosan natural polymer and used magnetized rGO (rGO-Fe3−xO4) as a filler to develop a bionanocomposite with hyperthermia capability. Compared with pristine chitosan, the antioxidant activity was 9 times higher and mechanical properties were elevated from (∼ 8 MPa) for chitosan to (∼ 285 MPa) for the bionanocomposite. Also, magnetic hyperthermia tests exhibited temperature raise up to 40 °C in 45 s with 50% of magnetized rGO which is relevant to the presence of rGO in the structure [106].

Giving the final target tissue or application, the scaffold polymers could be either natural or synthetic [107]. A. Zárate et al. used both natural and synthetic polymers to prepare polymeric electrospun fibers and loaded them with thermally rGO (TrGO) for hyperthermia evaluation. Under NIR laser irradiation, electrospun meshes (EM) of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), natural rubber (NR) and Arabic gum (AG) which has been modified with TrGO, reached the temperature of 42–45 °C and exhibited elevated photothermal ability in comparison with unmodified polymers. This research showed that certain natural or synthetic polymers could be used as potential candidates for hyperthermia application, once incorporated with a responsive agent such as photo-responsive rGO [108]. In a different study, Alkhayal et al. prepared the Fe3O4/rGO/poly-(ethylene) glycol (PEG) nanocomposite for magnetic hyperthermia application. Superparamagnetic iron oxide NPs aggregation was prevented by being dispersed on the surface of the rGO sheets with high surface area. magnetic hyperthermia was observed with the specific absorption rate of 58.33 W/g in an acidic solution [109].

In combination with anticancer drugs, hyperthermia can act even more efficiently in treating cancer. Hyperthermia enhances anticancer drug therapeutic capability with synergistic effects. Ren et al. combined magnetic hyperthermia as an efficient therapeutic method for malignant tumors and cancer treatment with the anticancer drug doxorubicin hydrochloride (DXR). They designed GO/Fe3O4-DXR nanocomposite and tested its ability under 80 kHz, 30 kAm-1) AMF. This composite showed promising capability in a combined drug release and magnetic hyperthermia application [110]. In another research study. Cui et al. designed lipid-bilayer-coated rGO modified-silica nanosheets as a nanodrug carrier for doxorubicin (DOX) with a high drug loading capacity of 53.3% and synergic photothermal therapy (PTT) capability. Under NIR irradiation, rGO generated heat and exhibited light-induced hyperthermia and triggered lipid bilayer phase transition with on-demand DOX release which resulted in solid tumor elimination in vivo [111].

The drug’s ability to penetrate the tumor site is a significant issue in chemotherapy. Limitation in infiltration could lead to low drug concentration and a decrease in chemotherapeutic results in monotherapy methods. Moreover, it could even cause tumor resistance to anticancer drug and promote metastasis [112]. Wang et al. developed iRGD-modified GO nanosheets (IPHG) and loaded it with DOX for chemo-photothermal combinational therapy. iRGD as a molecular mimicry agent aided with tumor penetration and targeted drug delivery, while GO nanosheets took part as a laser responsive agent for PTT. The IPHG-DOX was tested both in vitro and in vivo, and results indicated that this composite could penetrate the 4T1 tumor tissue and then, under NIR irradiation, combat tumor via synergistic PTT and drug release with elevated drug efficiency [113].

Osteosarcoma is a drug-resistance malignant bone tumor in children which results in chemotherapy failure in most cases. In a research study, Zeng et al. conjugated the mitochondria-targeting ligand (4-carboxybutyl) triphenyl phosphonium bromide (TPP) with indocyanine green (ICG)-loaded polyethyleneimine-modified PEGylated GO sheets and prepared (TPP-PPG@ICG) for enhanced mitochondrial accumulation and photodynamic-phototherapy synergic effects under a single dose NIR irradiation. The mitochondrial targeting nanocomposite with fluorescence imaging-guided phototherapy, showed tumor progress inhibition capability in MG63 tumor cells in vivo [114]. Another common type of cancer in the world is non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC). Cases with high-risk primary and metastasis of this type of cancer mostly demand multimodal therapies [115,116]. Coste-Almeida et al. prepared PEGylated reduced nanoGO (rGOn-PEG) for NMSC photothermal therapy. Compared to unmodified GOn, rGOn-PEG exhibited around 13-fold higher absorbance under NIR irradiation. 150 mW/cm2 NIR irradiation on the composite, led to temperature raise up to 47 °C which is considered as a mild temperature range in PTT. Also, rGO dispersibility was elevated by PEG modification. Around 38% decrease in A-431 cell were observed after an single NIR LED irradiation within 30 min which illustrates an NIR mild PTT on cancerous cells [117].

In order to achieve advanced photothermal hyperthermia capability in a composite, different light-responsive particles can be mixed for synergistic effects. In this regard, He et al. combined the two known NIR responsive particles, spherical gold nanoshells and GO and developed GO@SiO2 @AuNS hybrid composite for photothermal hyperthermia application. Under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation, the composite shows photothermal conversion efficiency of 30% and colorectal cancer cells (KM12C) elimination. At the first step, GO was covered with a mesoporous silica layer in order to develop binding sites for Au NPs. A seed-growing method was used for Au nanosheet growth. GO@SiO2 @AuNS hybrid exhibited a high photothermal conversion and biocompatibility [118].

In a different study, Moloudi et al. designed a multifunctional hybrid composite based on GO, iron oxide and gold NPs for cancer therapy via thermos-radiotherapy. The composite showed a time and dose-dependent temperature increase once it is exposed to radiofrequency waves. Maximum anticancer performance (53% cell death) was demonstrated once the nanohybrid was combined with radiofrequency waves and radiotherapy. In addition, the nanohybrids toxicity against MCF-7 cells was negligible [119].

There have been even studies for utilizing two different hyperthermia methods [120,121]. Yuan et al. designed a composite based on GO photothermal and Fe3O4 NPs magnetic capability. The magnothermal hydrogel composite consisting of thermosensitive poly (N-acryloyl glycinamide) (PNAGA), anticancer drug DOX, polyester (PE) capped mesoporous silica (MSNs) and Fe3O4 NPs grafted GO was prepared for combinational therapy of malignant tumors. The final composite (DMP), was tested both in vivo and in vitro and exhibited a well-controlled chemotherapy and magnothermal hyperthermia synergistic effect on tumor cells in mice models [122]. More examples are given on Table 1.

CNTs are formed of single, double, or multi-layers of graphite, which are rolled up to constitute cylindrical molecules. In the layers of CNTs, the hybridization of carbon atoms is sp2 and they form numerous hexagonal rings together. The CNTs delocalized electrons not only lead to electrical conductivity but also generates heat by absorbing certain light wavelengths. In addition to the optical, electrical, and thermal properties of CNTs, the other features such as mechanical, large surface area, high stability, easy functionalization, and high cellular penetration make CNTs good candidates in hyperthermia therapy, sensing and heat-generating textiles applications [150,151].

Recently, several studies have been performed in the field of cancer therapy using CNTs through hyperthermia techniques and investigating its multiple mechanisms. For instance, Abdi Goushbolagh et al. studied the anti-cancer effects of multi-walled CNT (MWCNTs)-COOH under NIR irradiation at different exposure times. The cell viability of CT26 fibroblastic cells was significantly decreased at 80 μg/mL concentration of modified MWCNTs under 1 min of irradiation while it is not toxic on its own [152].

Appropriate functionalization of CNTs related to their applications, which is carried out by different reactions, affects the optical properties and performance of light-to-heat conversion. In 2020, Nagai et al. synthesized anti-TRP-1-conjugated single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs) without changes in NIR absorption efficiency for melanoma cell treatment. Although the anti-TRP-1 was connected to SWCNT through strong covalent bonds between maleimide and thiol groups (ene-thiol reaction), its absorption performance is not affected ( Fig. 1) [153].

For improving the biocompatibility and water dispersibility of CNTs in hyperthermia therapy, they were modified by different hydrophilic polymers with many hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine functional groups [154]. Hereof, Asrar et al. developed polyethylene glycolated CNTs for investigation of their anti-cancer effect after excitation by NIR light. Compared to photosensitive TiO2 nanoparticles, the CNTs caused high tumor necrosis under laser irradiation due to the higher plasmonic properties and more heat generation [155].

Increasing the temperature by the photothermal method also has an effect on the release of loaded drugs in the desired environment. Li et al. evaluated the release of DOX from alkaline phosphatase-coated CNTs@ large pore mesoporous silica hydrogel upon NIR exposure. By increasing the amount of CNTs in the gel, the rate of temperature rises and drug release increases, therefore, hyperthermia can be used in drug delivery application, which is an important therapeutic method in cancer treatment [156].

Control of drug release via external stimuli prevents non-specific distribution and enables localized delivery. In this regard, Gangrade et al. developed a photo-electroactive silk@SWCNT-FA/DOX hydrogel in order to trigger the therapeutic agent on demand. In vivo experiment shows the electric field and NIR laser increase tumor apoptosis by inducing hyperthermia without any side effects. The optical feature and electrical conductivity of SWCNTs provide spatiotemporal controlled release of chemotherapeutics [157].

One of the challenges of cancer treatment is precise targeting which leads to therapeutic effects on cancer cells without compromising on healthy tissues. In a proof of concept, Wang et al. developed a pH-responsive system consisting of chitosan/SWCNTs decorated with PEG derivative. In addition to the mitochondrial targeting capacity of this nanoplatform, the ability to absorb NIR and mild hyperthermia enables the eradication of tumor cells by damaging mitochondria and inducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) burst inside the cell [158].

Hyperthermia can also be used as adjunctive therapy with other cancer treatment methods for enhancing therapeutic outcomes. For instance, Wang et al. introduced CNT@MnO2-PEG@Ce6 for MRI-guided photodynamic and photothermal synergistic therapy. In addition to heat generation by CNTs for carrying out hyperthermia, they delivered cargoes (Ce6 and MnO2) to cancer cells due to their high cellular penetration. The photodynamic therapy (PDT) was performed by oxidation of H2O2 in the tumor microenvironment and producing ROS under NIR irradiation [159]. In another research of combined therapy, hyperthermia has been used as an auxiliary method for chemodynamic therapy. Liu et al. introduced a PEG-MWNTs-Fe3O4-GOx nanoplatform for killing MCF-7 cancerous cells through increasing cell apoptosis. Mild hyperthermia accelerated the Fenton reaction activity under an 808 nm laser irradiation, and as result enhanced hydroxyl radicals (•OH) production to promote chemodynamic therapy [160].

One of the advantages of using the NIR laser is its deep penetration in tissues to expose photothermal agents to reach the desired temperature. The in vivo experiments are performed by Chen et al. displayed that the electrospun polycaprolactone nanofibers containing MWCNTs-OH and all-trans retinoic acid significantly reduced the size of glioma stem tumor under NIR irradiation. In this research, hyperthermia accompanies differentiation therapy to prevent tumor recurrence and improve antitumor efficiency [161].

All three photothermal, photodynamic, and chemotherapy could be carried on by different nanocomposites containing CNTs due to their unique physicochemical properties. For instance, Zhang et al. synthesized multifunctional SWCNTs-PEG-Fe3O4 @CQDs-DOX-Apt for combing mentioned treatments. This nanocomposite targeted lung cancer cells, induced photodynamic and photothermal effects, and release DOX following NIR irradiation. The high surface of CNTs causes to place of all these agents to accomplish these therapy methods simultaneously [162].

Thermal conductivity is another important physicochemical property of CNTs that must be considered in the hyperthermia treatment of tumors. In this regard, Ali J et al. used Yang’s and Maxwell’s model to calculate the thermal conductivity of the bio-nano fluid (plasma-CNT + red blood cells). The obtained result indicated that the thermal conductivity depended on the diameter of MWCNTs and SWCNTs, as well as the gender of used hematocrits [163].

In addition to the ability of CNTs to absorb NIR light, they also show a thermal response to radio frequency electromagnetic waves [16]. Recently, Morikawa et al. applied the radio frequency field to heat electrospun polymer nanocomposite fibers remotely. The applied CNTs within the fibers act as radio frequency receptor/heat sources to enhance the temperature of the nanocomposites. The fiber core temperature can exceed the melting temperature, while its surface temperature did not increase that much. The temperature distribution of the fibers was dependent on nanocomposite thermal conductivity. These radio frequency-sensitive fibers can be used in the biomedical and textile industrial applications such as hyperthermia treatment and heat-generating textiles [164].

Due to the electrical and thermal conductivity of CNTs, they were also utilized as multifunctional bioelectronic devices. Recently, Dinh et al. introduced a flexible polyacrylonitrile-CNT-polyacrylonitrile platform with thermal flow sensitivity. This porous platform can be used for monitoring human respiration [165].

The incorporation CNTs with various metal nanoparticles enhances the magnetic inductive heating performance in magnetic hyperthermia applications [166]. In this regard, Shen et al. synthesized magnetic Mn0.5Zn0.5Fe2O4/MWCNTs with various contents of MWCNTs by co-precipitation method. The computed SAR value and temperature changes indicated that the composite with the higher amount of MWCNTs (2 wt% and 4 wt%) has better efficiency in the low-frequency range of magnetic fields (238 ∼ 260 kHz). From in vitro experiments, it can be concluded that the MCF-7 cells were damaged under 10 min of hyperthermia magnetic treatment, while the cancer cells survive without the magnetic field ( Fig. 2) [167].

The proportion of metal used in nanoparticles also affects the magnetic properties of nanocomposites and ultimately their hyperthermia applications. Recently, Dalal et al. introduced NiZnCoFe2O4-MWCNT as an AC induction heat mediator as well as a good microwave absorber. The used different ratios of metal atoms demonstrate that the high percentage of Co improves the magnetic properties of the nanocomposite through increasing the anisotropy, and also the MWCNTs enhance the ability of the nanocomposite to absorb the microwave electromagnetic region. Furthermore, the applied MWCNTs are appropriate substrates to place nanoparticles due to their special structure and large surface area [168].

One of the disadvantages of using metal nanoparticles individually in hyperthermia treatment is their accumulation in an aqueous solution [169], hence correlating them with CNTs overcomes this detriment. Hereof, Seal et al. investigated the effective anisotropy and heat generation capability of PEG and amine-functionalized MWCNT/MnFe2O4 at two different concentrations. Compared to bare MnFe2O4, the functionalized CNTs enhance the dispersibility of these nanocomposites and also increases the saturation magnetization. Moreover, the evaluated SAR/ILP shows that these two nanocomposites are good candidates for magnetic hyperthermia treatment [170]. In a similar research work, Tiwaree et al. investigated the magnetic heating efficiency of three different functionalized MWCNT/MnFe2O4 nanocomposites. The value of SAR proves that the –COOH functionalized MWCNT/MnFe2O4 shows high magnetic heating performance compared to PEG and amine-functionalized MWCNT/MnFe2O4 [171].

The extraordinary magnetic properties of metal nanoparticles can also be considered in mixed therapy. For this purpose, Saghatchi et al. developed Fe3O4-MWCNT/AuNPs as a multifunctional tool for cancer therapy. The sensitivity of MWCNT and gold nanoparticles to radio waves and X-rays provided simultaneous hyperthermia and radiation therapy. Besides, this synthesized nanocomposite can be traced by ultrasound, CT scan, and MRI which can also be used in bioimaging applications [72].

In addition to the use of metal nanoparticles in magnetic hyperthermia therapy, they are also applied as a photothermal conversion agent to augment the photo-hyperthermia efficiency of CNTs [57]. In this regard, Saber-Samandari et al. synthesized gelatin/akermanite/Fe3O4/MWCNT nanocomposite scaffold for bone tissue engineering and hyperthermia. The mechanical properties of scaffold play a critical role in cancerous bone tumors treatment, so MWCNTs containing magnetic nanoparticles, not only improved the mechanical properties of the scaffold but also killed tumor cells by increasing temperature to above 43 °C under NIR laser irradiation [172].

Today, plasmonic noble metal nanoparticles such as Ag NPs have attracted the attention of scientists due to their high ability in converting the NIR and visible light into heat through surface plasmon resonance. Behnam et al. synthesized CNT/Ag NPs for plasmonic photothermal therapy in melanoma tumors. The histopathological results of tumor tissues showed extensive necrosis (70%) when using CNT/Ag NPs under NIR irradiation, while the extent of necrosis is lower when CNTs or Ag NPs used alone. As a result, the integration of CNTs with silver improved the optical absorption of CNTs significantly [173].

In summary, CNTs are a new generation of carbon-based nanomaterials that are widely used in various temperature-related applications such as cancer therapy, sensing and heat generating textiles. The delocalized electrons in these compounds are excited under various stimuli (e.g., light, microwave, and radio frequency) to heat up surrounding. The unique physicochemical properties of CNTs such as high surface area, easy functionalization and thermal conductivity enable integration with other photothermal and magnetic agents to carry out photo- and magnetic-hyperthermia. More examples of CNTs use in hyperthermia application are given in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots and carbon quantum dots (CDs &CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs and CQDs, newfangled material in the carbon clan, have possessed remarkable consideration due to their superb and tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, lofty quantum yield (QY), commendable biocompatibility, excellent optical properties, small scale, high capability of incorporation to distinct ingredients and plentiful low-cost sources, It offers momentous applications in many fields, including biomedicine, Optical electronic devices, Catalytic performance, etc. [190].

CDs have intrinsic fluorescence properties and efficient electron transport capabilities [191]. Using this property in one research, after the preparation of sulfur-CDs and growing Co9S8 at the nucleation site, heterostructure induction was done. The presence of CD in Co9S8/S-CDs@PEG (CSCs@PEG) nanoheterostructures has caused more ROS production and higher nanoenzymatic activity by filling the energy difference between excitation light and the bond gap with the generated thermal energy. The combination of PTT, PDT, Chemotherapy (CTD) and Starvation therapy methods in in vitro and in vivo tests have shown excellent anti-cancer results. Excretion was done through feces and urine within 2 weeks. It was announced that after 14 days, it can be roughly reported that the tumor has disappeared ( Fig. 3) [192].

In their study on colloidal CD nanoparticles with luminescent properties, Mussabek et al. proved that in the presence of light and radio frequency caused by an electromagnetic field, the resulting heat can be well controlled. This ability to control the appropriate temperature, along with biocompatibility, makes it an excellent factor for use in cancer treatment with PTT and PDT methods. [193].

Emissive CQDs are used in medical applications such as cell counting or sorting, multicolor bioimaging, and clinical diagnostics due to their optical stability and proper intracellular distribution along with biocompatibility [194]. Moniruzzaman et al. prepared polyaromatic-polyphenolic CQDs based on wet chemistry. The preparation method was quick and easy by a thermal process. The synthesized material enabled intense multicolor imaging of cells (yellow (YCQDs), blue (B-CQDs) and green (G-CQDs)). This was done in the entire range of visible light. Its photothermal efficiency PCE ∼ 32.6 ± 1% was reported. Further, the increase in ROS caused by the excellent photothermal conversion showed excellent photothermal antibacterial properties, which caused damage to the membrane of Bacillus subtilis ( Fig. 4) [195].

In another research by them, Perylene-CDs have also been introduced as an excellent absorber of NIR light at 808 nm with a lofty photothermal conversion efficiency (56.7%) and as a bioimaging guiding agent with high light-sensitive bactericidal efficiency. This nanoparticle has a greenish yellow fluorescent property, at 538 nm, it has shown a polychromatic emission maximum, which makes it an excellent bio-imaging without the need for metal. Besides, due to the increase in ROS caused by hyperthermia, its bactericidal activity has increased, which is very significant for use against drug-resistant bacteria [196].

In recent investigation, the effects of light stimulation on the treatment of bacterial infection have been confirmed as a safe and effective method [197]. CDs can absorb NIR light, and because of that, they can be used in PTT [198]. In this regard, He et al. have introduced a combination including CD and Ti called C-TiO2 NR. The CD present in the structure under visible light (VL) and NIR irradiation at 660 nm and 808 nm, respectively, has improved the photocatalytic and photothermal capabilities of the synthesized nanorods. In the in vivo and in vitro assays, the potential bacterial activity has been confirmed due to the combined effect of hyperthermia and ROS along with the rod morphology of the structure. Also, suitable adhesion and proper differentiation of bone mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) have been reported in the presence of these nanorods [199].

Polyphenol (MP)-CDs coordinated with manganese (Mn) under NIR (808 nm) with a particle size of 5 nm, along with biocompatibility and good dispersibility in water, along with high efficiency of photothermal conversion, has induced apoptosis and damage to cancer cells. Cancer cell hyperthermia has occurred through the release of ATP and AMGB1 along with the promotion of maturation and antigen presentation of dendritic cells (DCs) in vitro. However, the immune system could hardly prevent metastasis because its activation was not enough. This report promises the use of CDs in medical fields in a functional way [200]. CDs can be synthesized in various ways with variant crude materials [201,202]. Now, when ethylenediamine and citric acid are used to prepare them, amine and carboxyl groups are visible in the products, which are suitable sites for chemical reactions [203]. In the same case, after preparing CDs and then synthesizing Fe3O4-CDs by in situ hydrothermal process, Su et al. obtained magnetic nanoparticles with a size of 9 nm. Then, these nanoparticles were placed on carbon nanohorn treated with acid in situ, through sedimentation. This composite, which was prepared to investigate the drug delivery performance of 2 drugs, DOX and gemcitabine, under laser light irradiation and magnetic field, had convenient properties, including good dispersibility in water and superior magnetism. This composite has been introduced as a potential agent for use in simultaneous chemotherapy and hyperthermia [204].

Thermal treatment is a factor to which the bacterial membrane is sensitive. For this reason, the use of a material with a high photothermal conversion ability along with these materials shows an antibacterial synergistic effect [205]. In this context, Chu et al. conducted research on quaternary ammonium salt (QAS) along with CDs that were sensitive to NIR radiation at 808 nm. In the synthesized RCD-C35 composite, QAS caused the initial damage to the bacteria membrane and then CDs with photothermal conversion of 35%, revealed synergistic antibacterial attributes against E. coli (99.5%) and S. aureus (99.8%). In addition, its capability as a wound dressing and biological imaging in in vivo and in vitro conditions has made it a suitable factor for use in medical research fields [206]. Effective treatment by PTT method with mild temperature is far important for minimal damage to healthy cells and tissues [207]. Eke, lofty temperature is needed to destroy cell autophagy, which as a self-protection mechanism reduces the sensitivity of tumor cells to treatment [208].

Besides these cases, lysosomal escape of nanoparticles is also one of the weakening factors for the digestion and destruction of nanoparticles by lysosome and increasing the impressive effect of PTT [209]. Having the fluorescence virtues of nanoparticles plus the ability to absorb light and photothermal conversion is very helpful for surveillance and following these steps, that CDs provide this feature [210]. In this regard, PEG-PEI/CDs-E64d nanoagents synthesized with a mild temperature of 42 ℃ in 9 days by in vivo test have shown 84.3% tumor inhibition. simultaneously to inhibiting autophagy of tumor cells, these nanoparticles also had the capability of lysosomal escape. The sufficiency of lysosomal escape was investigated by staining red fluorescence of lysosome and nanoparticles with green fluorescence. At first, the overlapping of green and red fluorescence indicated the major distribution of nanoparticles in the lysosome. After a few hours of incubation, the intensity of separation of green and red fluorescence was observed. Behind several hours of incubation, the intensity of separation of green and red fluorescence was observed, and this increase indicates the suitability of this capability in nanoparticles [211].

GQDs are carbon nanoparticles with zero dimensions [212]. They have superb photoluminescence attributes, by having the edge effect in addition to the quantum confinement [213]. These nanoparticles have been investigated as PDT agents for producing ROS in recent research in various fields [214–219]. In the bactericidal property, by using the synergistic effect of ROS production and heat generation, damage to drug-resistant gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria is done by destroying the membrane and cytoplasmic exudation [220]. This combined treatment is non-invasive. It also has a few side effects with low sensitivity and toxicity [221]. In this context, effective 3-mode antimicrobial treatment has been carried out by placing silver nanoparticles on GQDs and investigating its properties. This surface connection has caused ROS production and photothermal conversion to increase when nanoparticles are exposed to blue light with a wavelength of 450 nm. This effect took place at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL during 10 min of laser exposure. Further, the temperature increase was up to 40 degrees with a power density of 14 mW/cm2 [222].

Withal, compounds containing GQDs can be used in drug delivery systems [223]. In addition to ease of operation, high specific surface area and excellent photothermal conversion, these nanoparticles have the ability to prevent premature release of the drug as a gatekeeper during blood circulation. In addition to having favorable biocompatibility, they enhance tumor growth suppression [224]. Considering these capabilities, a nanoplatform with GQD, mesoporous carbon nanoparticle (HMCN) and hyaluronic acid (HA) components has been introduced. This nanoplatform for chemo-photothermal therapy with the ability to target drug delivery to cervical cancer cells with overexpression of the CD44 receptor has been investigated in vitro and in vivo. 410 mg/g of DOX drug was loaded into this structure. It can specifically target the desired cancer cells and can provide synergistic treatment with high photothermal conversion [225].

All the mentioned points indicate the very suitable capabilities of CDs and its various types in different applications, which promises progress in laser-based industries and the manufacture of light-sensitive agents using these nanoparticles. More examples are given in Table 3.

Other carbon-based nanomaterials used in hyperthermia application, are nanodiamonds, fullerene and carbon fiber. Nanodiamonds (NDs) exhibit high biocompatibility and anti-corrosive ability and can be used as fair candidates for transporting and delivery applications [257]. Nanodiamond is the most promising nanocarbon for biomedical application since studies indicated ND can be tolerated in mammal organisms [258]. In order to use NDs in biological environment however, a strict control on the particles size, alongside with hydrophilic functionalization is required [259]. Maziukiewicz et al. used photothermal agent polydopamine (PDA) and laser synthesized NDs as a fluorescent agent for photothermal therapy combined with fluorescent imaging. In vitro studies indicated the composites high potential for photothermal therapy of cancer under laser irradiation combined with bioimaging resulted from NDs optical property [260]. In a different study, Cheng et al. designed fluorescent ND-gold/silver NPs and prepared FND-Au/Ag-Tf NPs. The composite exhibited photo-stability and long-term cancer tracing capability. The composite was also found to be beneficial in cancerous cells labeling and photothermal therapy of cancer while labeling and targeting tumor cells [261].

The other carbon-based nanostructure that can be utilized in hyperthermia application in fullerene. C60 fullerene is considered as an interesting cancer therapy agent with ROS generation capability and photodynamic effect [262]. Even though fullerene has shown potential for microwave hyperthermia to overcome cancer, to overcome this particle’s poor solubility, different functionalization are needed. Sun et al. prepared fullerene C60 encapsulated in NP core consisting of poly (propylene glycole) (PPG) Pluronic F127 to avoid aggregation. pluronic F127-chitosan NPs (PCNPs) was used withC60 and the final composite of C60-PCNP was used on human prostate cancer cells in combination with microwave hyperthermia for an enhanced cancer therapy efficiency. Modified fullerene were taken up by cells and then adsorbed microwave heating, which enhanced hyperthermia and cancer therapy effectiveness [19].

Carbons can turn into fibrous structures by using different methods such as electrospinning. Fibers produced via electrospinning have gained attention in biomedical applications due to their multifunction properties such as high surface to volume ratio. Boutros et al. developed carbon nanofibers with metallic additives for biomedical applications specially hyperthermia. In order to prepare ultrathin fibers, electrohydrodynamic method was used which is a combination of electrospinning or spraying with casting for the preparation of poly-vinylpyrrolidone (PVP), polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers and PAN-derived polymeric carbon composite nanofibers. It was demonstrated that the heat-treated PAN-derived carbon composite nanofiber exhibit hyperthermia property once exposed to microwave. However, PAN polymer NFs exhibited better hyperthermia activity than carbon nanofibers when the exposure time is more than 20 s [263].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clinical and preclinical trials of the carbon-based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, extensive research endeavors have been undertaken to investigate the diverse attributes of carbon-based nanocomposites, encompassing their anti-cancer and antibacterial properties through in vitro assessments. The central issue revolves around how nanocomposites affect hyperthermia applications in both clinical and preclinical models. To date, many researches have been conducted on the application of hyperthermia in clinical models [264,265], as well as many preclinical studies have been done on carbon-based nanomaterials for the hyperthermia therapy [266,267]. For instance, the magnetic nanoparticles could potentially enhance the use of carbon-based nanocomposites for magnetic hyperthermia in a clinical translation. Recent research on clinical hyperthermia of prostate cancer using magnetic nanoparticles indicated that the nanocomposite based on carbon-based nanomaterials and magnetic nanoparticles with high thermal and dispersion properties can be evaluated in clinical translation [268,269], Moreover, in another research, the hyperthermia applications of magnetic fluid was investigated for treatment of glioblastoma multiforme tumors [270]. As a result, the carbon nanomaterials, which are used in nanocomposites along with magnetic nanoparticles, can be applied in clinical investigations.

According to previous investigations, preclinical studies lead to potential clinical translation. Recently, research on preclinical models has been conducted by Moon et al., showing that PEG-SWCNTs destroy mouse tumors through the implementation of photothermal therapy ( Fig. 5) [271]. This nanomaterial not only exhibited minimal toxicity and maximal biodegradability but also display complete destruction of the tumors without harmful side effects or recurrence of tumors over 6 months. In another preclinical research, the EMT6 tumor-bearing mice were treated with ox-MWCNTs in combination with local hyperthermia at 43 °C [265].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges in hyperthermia and related materials used
ce:section.ce:para: The first major challenge in using carbon-based nanomaterials in biomedical applications is their biosafety issues and related considerations. The biocompatibility and biodegradability of these particles strongly depend on their size, synthetic method, surface functionalization and hydrophobicity. [34]. Therefore, using green materials and methods for their preparation, proper surface functionalization and using polymer matrixes for immobilization of these particles can be beneficial in improving their biosafety in biomedical applications [272]. Even though hyperthermia has proven to be beneficial in cancerous and bacteria cells destruction, in some cases it might lead to burn injuries. As reported, the most challenging issue in the use of magnetic hyperthermia is the increase of nanoparticles heating power and controlling the local temperature of the tumor site [273]. Also, magnetic nanoparticles used in the magnetic hyperthermia might exhibit cytotoxicity toward healthy cells. This challenge can be minimized using modification. Moreover, magnetic nanoparticles size influences their cytotoxicity. Studies have indicated that magnetic particles show more toxicity toward chicken macrophages when they are sized between 60 and 250 nm [274]. But no significant toxicity was observed on HeLa cells and postnatal human fibroblasts with 30 µm magnetic particle size [275]. In addition, magnetic nanoparticles cytotoxicity can be reduced by creating core-shell structures using polymeric matrixes such as hydrogels [276]. Other than magnetic hyperthermia, photo-induced hyperthermia also faces challenges in clinical applications. The most concerning challenge in photothermal therapy is the depth of light penetration which might lead to incomplete tumor cure [277]. The best solution to overcome this challenge is combination therapy and achieving synergistic treatment effects [278]. To conclude, hyperthermia therapy is a beneficial way for destruction of cancerous and bacteria cells. However, for clinical applications, important factors such as dose, nanoparticles sizes and heat distribution and also, the choose of the hyperthermia method must be considered in order to avoid treatment failure or unintended side effects.

In this study, a carbon structure-based review on these interesting particles application in hyperthermia therapy of cancerous and bacteria cells destruction is presented. Carbon-based nanostructures consisting of GO, CNT, CD, CQD, ND, fullerene and CF with good physicochemical stability, thermal conductivity and optical properties can be combined with a variety other material such as hydrogel-based composites, magnetic nanoparticles and etc. for elevated performance in the hyperthermia target environment. To conclude, carbon-based nanostructures have natural responsivity towards some hyperthermia methods, for example GO responsivity to optical hyperthermia. Their ease of functionalization however, enables their usage in hyperthermia methods in which they are not naturally active. These properties, make carbon nanomaterials good candidates for hyperthermia application. Discovering newer methods for overcoming carbon-based nanostructures hydrophobicity and also, greener approaches for their synthesis can be considered in future studies.
